title: Inside Xi's System: The New Face of Totalitarianism
content:
Totalitarianism typically features three salient elements: a grand project to remake society, the total politicization of private life, and rule by terror through secret police. Communist totalitarianism also traditionally includes an economic element involving the abolition of private property and comprehensive public ownership. Chinese President Xi Jinping’s system resembles these classic definitions in parts, but it currently lacks the sustained mass mobilization seen in the Mao era. A more accurate description of the current regime is that it functions as an intensified autocracy. It represents a technological and organizational reinforcement of traditional authoritarian rule updated for the digital age.

This new autocracy manifests as finer-grained social control, tighter discipline imposed on the bureaucracy, and a highly centralized orchestration of policy and propaganda. It certainly imposes immense political pressure and actively suppresses independent thought and speech across the nation. However, its primary goal is to aggressively remake and purify the Chinese Communist Party itself, rather than attempting to reshape human nature. This internal remaking is visible most fiercely in the People's Liberation Army (PLA), where Xi has implemented a deeply personalized arrangement of power. The setup inside the once relatively closed-off military system now closely approaches totalitarianism.

At the center of this military transformation is the Central Military Commission (CMC) chairman responsibility system, which Xi introduced in 2014. This system emphasizes the chairman’s absolute and total control over all military matters, elevating it to the military’s highest political principle. Through strict ideology, rigid rules, and pervasive accountability, it penetrates every part of the PLA, personalizing the party’s absolute leadership. The endpoint of loyalty is no longer an abstract political party but the chairman himself; the endpoint of obedience is personal judgment rather than collective decision-making. Although elegant in its design, implementing such absolute personal leadership inside a vast military inevitably runs into structural contradictions.

The fundamental problem is that the chairmanship system requires all command and management to be unified under one individual, which is practically impossible. Xi lacks the time, bandwidth, and highly specialized expertise needed to carry out day-to-day command and managerial functions in areas like training and equipment readiness. As a result, his role is closer to that of a supreme decision-maker, while the daily operation of the military is delegated to trusted CMC vice chairmen. Xi wants these vice chairmen to be mere handlers who seamlessly carry out his instructions without encroaching on his power. However, these proxies naturally assert authority themselves, using their professional expertise to selectively implement or quietly resist the chairman’s orders.

This selective implementation does not necessarily mean subversion, but it does create a secondary power center around the vice chairmen. As these proxies become more powerful, they develop their own personnel networks through their recommendation power and de facto vetoes over appointments. The chairman instinctively perceives this secondary center of power as a major threat to his absolute leadership system. Military power ceases to be a direct line from the chairman to the troops, acquiring a problematic circuit breaker in between. This dynamic explains why Xi’s anti-corruption campaign continuously targets higher levels of the military, serving as a tool to dismantle unauthorized patron networks.
